title: Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and food availability, plotting average vegetable oil supply per capita in kilograms per year against gross domestic product per capita on a logarithmic axis. The horizontal axis spans from $2,000 to $100,000 in international dollars at 2021 prices, while the vertical axis ranges from 0 to 35 kilograms. Points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The distribution shows a general positive association where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher vegetable oil supply, though the spread is wide. At the lower end of the economic scale, countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo and Niger sit below $3,000 GDP with oil supplies under 5 kilograms per person. Moving toward the middle of the income spectrum, nations such as Jordan, Belize, and Iraq cluster between $10,000 and $20,000 GDP, displaying oil supplies that range from roughly 10 to 15 kilograms. Several countries deviate from the central trend; Brazil and Algeria appear above the main cluster with supplies near 25 kilograms despite moderate GDP levels, while Denmark and Norway sit at the far right with GDPs exceeding $60,000 but oil supplies below 10 kilograms. High-income nations including the United States, Canada, and Australia occupy the upper-right quadrant, combining GDPs over $50,000 with supplies between 20 and 30 kilograms. The data points for Europe and North America generally occupy the higher ranges of both axes, whereas African nations predominantly populate the lower-left section of the chart. The visual pattern indicates that vegetable oil availability increases with economic output up to a point, after which the relationship becomes less consistent among the wealthiest economies.
